cultural_notes: §1.2.1.1 Tribal groups: Kuku Yalanji society is closely associated with land. Six groups or 'mobs' are identified with particular territorial tracts focused on river drainage areas (Anderson 1980:78). Within each mob are descent groups associated with patrilineally inherited 'countries' (bubu), named by their country, e.g. banabila-warra 'inhabitants of Banabila', wujalwujal-warra 'inhabitants of Wujal Wujal'. The derivation -warra means 'inhabitants of' (§3.2.3.7).
§1.2.1.1: Descent groups care for their bubu, which usually contains one or more sites of spiritual/mythological significance called yirmbal 'story place'.

§1.2.1.1: Ecological self-designations distinguish dwellers: majaji 'with rainforest' vs ngalkalji 'with open woodland'; jalunji 'with sea' vs bana yiralji 'with fresh water'. These are comitative (-ji) derivations.

§1.2.1.2 Personal names: Aboriginal names mostly held by people over ~60; people are reticent to disclose them and rarely called openly by native names. Native-language nicknames in general use, e.g. jina-baji 'sore foot' (jina 'foot') for a man whose foot was injured by a stonefish as a boy.

§1.2.1.3 Social structure: Each Yalanji/Nyungkul speaker belongs to one of two moieties: walarr or dabu — both also names of bee species. Main totems: birrmba 'white cockatoo' (walarr) and kumurrbina 'black cockatoo' (dabu) (W. Oates & L. Oates 1964a).

§1.2.1.3 Marriage: A correct marriage partner belongs to the opposite moiety; children belong to the same moiety as their father. Formerly a man could have as many wives as he could support, also providing for in-laws.
§1.2.1.3 Obligatory sharing: Each family member had an intrinsic right to a particular portion of available food, ensuring provision for the elderly or sick; now extended to money supplies.

§1.2.1.3 Avoidance: Total avoidance between a man and his mother-in-law (no speaking, not in same room); less strict taboos on other in-law relationships (may converse but not touch). buliman 'policemen' (English loan) is cited. An avoidance language was formerly used among certain in-law relations but is now lost (no records); cf. Guugu Yimidhirr brother-in-law avoidance style (Haviland 1979a,b).

§1.2.1.4 Food: Rainforest and coastal strip gave abundant supplies: fruit/nuts (bush cherry, blue quandong, wild apple, figs, zamia nut, Moreton Bay chestnut), roots (yams, wild ginger), wild honey, smaller mammals (possums, bandicoot), birds and eggs (scrub hen, cassowary, Torres Strait pigeon), goannas, pythons (poisonous snakes NOT eaten), fresh- and saltwater turtles, fish, shellfish, crustaceans, insects (green ant, witchetty grub).

§1.2.1.4 Food preparation: zamia nut and hairy yam needed elaborate prep — roasting, pounding into flour, leaching in water for hours; zamia leaching removes a poisonous substance, hairy yam soaking removes unpleasant taste. Meat/fish roasted in a ground oven between hot stones and leaves (still used for freshwater eels on 'walkabout'). Boiling in a bark trough or large shell was traditional to the Bloomfield River region (unknown elsewhere in North Queensland).
§1.2.1.4: Native mammals, cassowaries and pythons have all but disappeared since the introduction of the poisonous cane toad; new food resources are mangoes and wild pigs.

§1.2.1.4 Cannibalism: not unknown but only practised under hunger; the victim was not killed for the purpose but eaten after death by natural causes or in a fight; no special term or ceremony (Roth 1901:30). Palmer River gold-rush stories: Chinese regarded as better eating than Europeans; a slain enemy's skull fashioned into a billycan (informant Charlie Tayley; Anderson & Mitchell 1981).

§1.2.1.5 Artefacts: Different types of dillybags and traditional hunting weapons such as fish and turtle spears are still made and used, but metal has replaced traditional materials for spear prongs and hooks.

§1.2.1.6 Medicine: Minor ailments treated with bush medicine, e.g. yangka 'green ant (juice)' for fever, colds and stomach upsets; poultices from stewed bark of kabal 'Leichardt tree' or leaves of jujubala 'ironwood tree' for rheumatic pain. Severe/chronic illness handled by the rrunyuji (also spelled rrunyunji) or murri doctor, who would extract 'by magic' a 'thing' (usually a stingray barb, later a marble) believed to be the cause of illness.

§1.2.1.7 Taboos: Food from certain places may be taboo — game/fish from the north-eastern part of Weary Bay is believed to bring severe illness (cancer, paralysis) and must not be eaten. Food collected by certain family members is taboo to certain siblings: a woman should not eat meat/fish obtained by her younger brother; a man should not eat certain plant foods and eggs gathered by his younger sister.

§1.2.1.7 Death/name taboo: Mention of a deceased person's name was totally proscribed while the body was decomposing (corpse wrapped in bark, placed in a tree over a fire until only bones left, carried by family then buried). The substitute term ngalba replaced the name for the deceased and living persons of the same name; now ngalba + name (e.g. 'ngalba Ivy') is used. ngalba is presumably cognate to Guugu Yimidhirr ngalba- 'hide, be hidden' (fn11). Recently deceased persons are talked about as little as possible; tape recordings of them must not be listened to.

§1.2.1.8 Other customs: Initiation practices (piercing of the nasal septum, front tooth extraction, 'marking' with cicatrices) have been discontinued over the last ~60 years. Persistent offenders (thieves, taboo-breakers) used to be speared, sometimes to death.

§1.2.1.8 Magic: kanyil 'curse song' — if wronged (e.g. adultery), a person would 'sing' the offender, believed to ultimately cause the culprit's death; last reported kanyil performed in the late 1930s (W. Oates & L. Oates 1964a). Death often attributed to sorcery; the rrunyunji would watch for the sorcerer's spirit (attracted by the dead person's belongings), and once identified the family had a duty to avenge by spearing.

§1.2.1.8 Weather/belief: Thunderstorms (early November to end of December) are greatly feared; trespassing on a sacred site is thought to bring a storm; burning ironwood leaves or turtle skin is believed to drive a storm away.
§1.2.1.8 Messenger birds: wakuka 'kookaburra' is thought to herald the death of a family member.

§1.2.1.8 Spirits: wuyngkul = spirit of a sick person close to death, moving about causing mischief (accidents, sudden pain), visible to gifted people, disappearing on the death/recovery of the sick person. wadalwadal = a water spirit with female body and fishtail, living in a spring in the Thompson Creek valley, associated with one particular Yalanji family clan; tolerates rightful family members who may use its healing spring water.

§1.2.1.8 Creation myths: kija 'moon' created women at kijandaku 'Roaring Meg Falls'; kurriyala 'carpet snake' died and is buried at mangkal 'Cedar Bay'; ngurrku 'mopoke'; bajabaja 'blue tongue lizard', whose eggs hatched in the dry Bloomfield River bed and turned into two prominent rocks. All rivers/creeks originate from the amniotic fluid of one of two sisters who gave birth at ngalbabulal 'Mount Pieter Botte' (between Bloomfield and Daintree).

§1.1.1: kuku means 'word, language'; the language name Kuku Yalanji = 'the language with "this"' (yala-nji 'this-COMIT'). Several dialect names comprise kuku (koko) + a characteristic marked by comitative -ji (§3.2.3.3).

§1.1.4 Creole: A Cape York Creole is in use, with features: -im on English transitive verb roots (put-im, chuck-im); -bala on adjectives (deadbala 'dead person', flash-bala 'distinguished', slightly derogatory); blonga/bla for possession (car blonga/bla Cedric); lexical items too much, big mob 'a lot', savvy 'know'.

§3.1.2: Generic nouns can stand alone or act as classifiers (§4.1.1(c)): bama 'Aboriginal person', dingkar 'male', jalbu 'female', jawun 'unspecified family member (now generally friend)', minya 'edible animal (incl. birds and fish)', dikal 'bird', jarba 'snake', kuyu 'fish', mayi 'edible plants (also meal/food in general)', juku 'tree (classifies anything wooden)', bubu 'country, ground, earth, space', kulji 'stone, rock, rock formation (not mountain)', bana 'water, liquid', muyar 'air, wind', baya 'fire (also firewood; classifies smoke, matches, hot ashes)', kalka 'spear', bayan 'camp, house, shelter', junjuy 'something, anything'.

§3.1.2 Colour: only three colour terms — ngumbu 'dark', bingaji 'light', ngalangala 'red, reddish'. bingaji also means 'old man' (presumably from white hair); ngalangala may be reduplicated, cf. mulamula 'bright red' from mula 'blood' (no analysable root ngala).

§3.1.2 Emotion/state compounds: jiba-badiy 'feel sorry, sad' (lit. liver-cry); wawulay 'feel exhausted' (wawu-wulay 'breath-die'). Bodily/mental-state nouns: warngku 'sleep', kaka 'pain', kuli 'anger', kuru '(female) love or lust'.
§3.1.2 Numbers: nyubun 'one', djambul 'two', kulurr 'three' (note: djambul OCR — orthography elsewhere likely jambul); plus general quantity terms (§3.2.5).
§3.1.2 / §4.10 Interjections: yuwu 'yes', kari 'no', plus general encouragement/agreement/disagreement.

§1.1.3 Territory/ecology: Three ecological zones — open woodland (west of the Great Dividing Range), dense rainforest (eastern slopes to the coast, permanent springs/creeks/rivers, abundant game/plant food/fish), and coastal plains (river mouths e.g. Bloomfield & Daintree; coastal thicket and mangrove swamps; plentiful fish and maritime fauna). Yalanji and Nyungkul traditionally inhabited the rainforest terrain where Wujal Wujal Community now sits.

phonological_rules: (§2.3(i)) Kuku Yalanji stops /b/ and /j/ correspond to English fricatives closest in place of articulation: /b/ corresponds to English /v, f/; /j/ corresponds to English /s, z, ʃ, ʒ/. Examples: naybu 'knife', aybi 'Ivy (name)', buji 'pussy (cat)', juka 'sugar', maji 'matches'.

(§2.3(ii)) English voiceless and aspirated stops become unaspirated and usually voiced. NOTE: orthographic k in Kuku Yalanji normally represents voiced [g]. Examples (OCR notes intended forms): dubayku 'tobacco', muduka/mudaka 'motorcar'. (Informant gloss: 'pussycat' -> buji/bujiga, not aspirated phussikhath.)

(§2.3(iii)) English /t/ and /s/ before a velar stop become Kuku Yalanji /rr/ (retroflex/trilled rhotic, written rr). Examples: marrkin 'musket (rifle)' (< musket), warrkin 'Watkins' (place name, < Watkins).

(§2.3(iv)) English consonant clusters consisting of a stop or fricative PLUS a sonorant are broken up by inserting a vowel. Examples: jarruja 'trousers', gilaja 'glass (mirror)', bulawu 'flour', banabul 'pineapple'. If an INITIAL cluster is broken up, the inserted vowel is stressed per the word-stress rule (§2.6.1), e.g. jarruja. The grammar states examples are too few to predict WHICH vowel is inserted.
(§2.3(iv), continued) An INITIAL fricative+stop consonant cluster is instead simplified by DROPPING the first consonant, e.g. buwun 'spoon' (< spoon; the only example in the data).

(§2.3(v)) English loans with an initial vowel are preceded by a semivowel: /w/ before a back vowel, /y/ before a front or central vowel. Examples: wulman 'old man' (< old man), yalibala 'early' (< early).

(§2.3(vi)) Lateral /l/ is the one normally-disallowed initial consonant that IS retained in loanwords, e.g. landin 'Landing' (place name). No loans with an original initial rhotic (r/rr) have been attested.

(§2.3(vii)) Monosyllabic English roots are made disyllabic, EITHER by inserting a semivowel sequence within the root (e.g. buwun 'spoon', biyin '(safety) pin'), OR by adding a final vowel if the original or adapted root ends in a stop (e.g. biki / bikibiki 'pig', juki 'chook (hen)', baybu 'pipe', naybu 'knife'). No generalisation on the choice of final vowel is possible.

(§2.3(viii)) Final consonants not permitted in Kuku Yalanji phonotactics are adapted by one of four means: (a) adding a vowel after an original or adapted stop (as in (vii) and gilaji 'glass', mijiji 'Mrs'); (b) changing a stop to the corresponding nasal (marrkin 'musket'); (c) changing a peripheral nasal to a non-peripheral one (landin 'Landing'); (d) simply dropping the final consonant (jarruja 'trousers', bujika 'pussycat', warrkin 'Watkins').

(§2.3(ix)) Vowel adaptation: data is insufficient for a full generalisation, except that Kuku Yalanji vowels on the whole approximate the English vowel, and English [ɛ] or [æ] and the diphthong [aɪ] before a consonant tend to become /ay/ in Kuku Yalanji, e.g. mayngku 'mango', dubayka 'tobacco', baybu 'pipe'.

(§2.3 general) Only about a score (~20) of English loans became an integral part of the lexicon. Phonological adaptation of loans largely CEASED about one generation before the grammar (Donaldson 1980:62) with rising bilingualism; younger speakers use English words (car, aeroplane, church, store, soft-drink) with English pronunciation but with Kuku Yalanji inflectional affixes (see §3.12). Only older speakers (~60+) still partially adapt newer words (e.g. telelzision [OCR for 'television'], job job for 'Cop Shop').

detection_heuristics: A word glossing a post-contact / European-introduced commodity (car, flour, sugar, knife, pipe, pig, hen, glass, trousers, tobacco, matches, etc.) is a candidate loan (§2.3).
Presence of /b/ where the English source has /v/ or /f/, or /j/ where English has /s/, /z/, /ʃ/, /ʒ/, strongly indicates a loan (§2.3(i)). E.g. naybu<knife, buji<pussy, maji<matches.
Voiced/unaspirated stops corresponding to English voiceless stops (English p,t,k -> Yalanji b,d,k[=g]) signal a loan; orthographic k = [g] (§2.3(ii)).
A medial -rr- corresponding to English /t/ or /s/ immediately before a velar stop suggests a loan (§2.3(iii)): marrkin<musket, warrkin<Watkins.

An epenthetic vowel splitting what is an English stop/fricative+sonorant cluster (e.g. -CVC- where English had a cluster) flags a loan: jarruja<trousers, gilaja<glass, bulawu<flour, banabul<pineapple (§2.3(iv)).

Initial w- before a back vowel or y- before a front/central vowel that has no native morphological motivation may be a prothetic semivowel on a vowel-initial English root: wulman<old man, yalibala<early (§2.3(v)).
A word-INITIAL lateral l- is anomalous for native Kuku Yalanji phonotactics and therefore points to a loan (§2.3(vi)): landin<Landing. (Word-initial rhotics do not occur in loans.)

Internal -Vw V- or -ViV- semivowel sequences, or a 'extra' final vowel after a stop, may mark a monosyllabic English root forced to be disyllabic: buwun<spoon, biyin<pin, biki<pig, juki<chook, baybu<pipe, naybu<knife (§2.3(vii)).
An /ay/ diphthong corresponding to English [ɛ], [æ], or [aɪ] before a consonant suggests a loan: mayngku<mango, dubayka<tobacco, baybu<pipe (§2.3(ix)).

Final -i or -a appearing as an apparent stem extension after what would be an English-final stop, or a final consonant changed to its corresponding nasal, points to phonotactic loan repair: gilaji<glass, mijiji<Mrs, marrkin<musket, landin<Landing (§2.3(viii)).

Morphological tells (§3.12): a -bala-suffixed adjective (drunk-bala, brown-bala, waybala) is a Pidgin-derived English loan; a noun bearing catalytic -mun- before case (car-mun-du) is an English loan stem; an English-looking verb root carrying -im- + -bunga-l/-ba-l (fix-im-bunga-l, push-im-bunga-l) or -ma-l / -mani-l is a borrowed English verb.

Beware false positives: native words semantically EXTENDED to new referents (kambi 'flying fox'->'clothes', diburr 'eggs'->'bullets', mayi karraji 'fruit with strings'->'mangoes') are NOT phonological loans (§2.3) and must not be tagged as English loans despite the European gloss.

Loans entering after ~1 generation before the grammar typically retain English pronunciation (no phonological adaptation) and surface only with native inflection; absence of the above phonological signatures does NOT rule out a recent loan (§2.3, §3.12).
loan.morphological_adaptation: (§3.12) English loans are integrated morphologically, sometimes with no phonological adaptation. (1) NOUNS: English noun roots of any syllable count usually take the CATALYTIC suffix -mun- before inflections or stem-forming suffixes, e.g. car-mun-du (LOC), school-mun-ku (DAT), motorbike-mun-ji (COMIT). The -mun- is NOT obligatory; loans are often attested without it, e.g. toyota-nga (LOC), Chris-angka (ERG, proper noun; OCR 'Chrisangka'), car-nga (LOC). (2) ADJECTIVES: English adjectives take -bala (a Pidgin feature, < 'fellow', cf. waybala 'white man'), e.g. drunk-bala, brown-bala, busy-bala-baja-ku 'very busy'. (3) VERBS: English verbs are borrowed as nominal roots then verbalised by inchoative or causative derivation. INTRANSITIVE verbs take the inchoative verbaliser -ma-l; if the root is monosyllabic the inchoative suffix is usually REDUPLICATED, e.g. travel-ma-l, shift-ma-l-ma-l. The verb 'work' often takes the causative suffix -mani-l (used with intransitive verbs, §3.8.5.1) but in passivised form with -ji-, e.g. ngayu work-ma-n-mani-ji-ny 'I worked (lit. I was made to work)'. TRANSITIVE verb roots take -im- (< Pidgin 'him', cf. 'fix him', Crowley & Rigsby 1979) before the transitive verbaliser -bunga-l ~ -ba-l, e.g. fix-im-bunga-l (~ fix-im-ba-l), push-im-bunga-l, count-im-bunga-l, polish-im-bunga-l. Only -bunga-l (NOT -kanga-l) is used with English verbs.
loan.notes:
SECTION CITATIONS: All phonological rules and loan forms are from §2.3 'Phonological adaptation of English loan words' (grammar.md lines 1043-1111); morphological adaptation is from §3.12 (lines 3969-3979). Patz draws the nine generalisations from Dixon 1972:326; see also Dixon, Ramson & Thomas 1990 ch.5 and Donaldson 1980:62.

OCR ARTEFACTS / RECONSTRUCTIONS (intended forms): 'orjiri-burr' (line 1045) likely an OCR garble of a native compound glossed 'through the sky' for 'aeroplane' (meaning-extension example, not a loan). 'IITI' / 'IIT/' in rule (iii) = the rhotic /rr/. 'Iwl'='/w/', 'Iyl'='/y/', 'layl'/'IayI'='/ay/', 'lsi'='/s/', 'It I'='/t/' (OCR of slashes as capital I/l). 'injarruja' (line 1110) = 'in jarruja' ('as in jarruja'). 'telelzision' = 'television'. Motorcar form appears variably as 'mudaka', 'muduka', and 'muduka' across the text; 'juka'/'juka' for sugar; 'dubayku'/'dubayka' for tobacco; 'gilaja' (mirror) vs 'gilaji' (glass) are both given. These spelling variants are in the source, not introduced here.

KEY DISTINCTION for the dictionary pipeline: §2.3 documents TWO post-contact naming strategies. (A) Meaning extension of native words (kambi, diburr, mayi karraji, the aeroplane compound) - these are NATIVE words and must NOT be classified as English loans even though their gloss names a European item. (B) Phonological adaptation of the English name (all the loan forms in attested_loans). Detection must separate these.

ABBREVIATIONS used: LOC=locative, DAT=dative, COMIT=comitative, ERG=ergative, pt=proper noun/personal (Patz's gloss for the Chris-angka ergative-proper form). The transitive verbaliser is -bunga-l ~ -ba-l (allomorphs); -im- < 'him' and -bala < 'fellow' are Pidgin/creole-derived (Crowley & Rigsby 1979).
